normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.source_section: MASVS-RESILIENCE: Resilience Against Reverse Engineering and Tampering — MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 (page 31)
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 (Description): Apps run on a user-controlled device, and without proper protections it's relatively easy to run a modified version locally (e.g. to cheat in a game, or enable premium features without paying), or upload a back-doored version of it to third-party app stores. This control tries to ensure the integrity of the app's intended functionality by preventing modifications to the original code and resources.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must implement anti-tampering mechanisms appropriate to the platform — code and resource integrity checks where supported, signed-build identity verification through platform-supported mechanisms (iOS code signing, Android signature scheme v2/v3), detection of modified or backdoored package distributions, and declared tamper-response policy (terminate, degraded mode, alert-and-continue). Anti-tampering complements platform integrity validation (RESILIENCE-1) and anti-static-analysis (RESILIENCE-3) as the runtime arm of defense-in-depth against modification of the shipped binary.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies at Tier 2+ as a conservative corpus-level gate. Per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime), anti-tampering discipline activates at Tier 2+ where the app handles content distribution and analytics; the primary trace SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-TAMPER is a Tier 2-5 control (new in Constitution v0.5). The blueprint schema does not currently declare a discrete tamper-response-policy field as a top-level applicability filter, so security_manifest.app_tier >= 2 is used as a broad proxy. If a Tier 2+ app explicitly accepts the threat-model risk of running a modified binary (e.g., an internal-only build, an open-source app with documented exception), auditors may mark the requirement not-applicable during evidence review with documented justification in security_manifest. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 2+ requires anti-tampering protections because content-distribution apps face the back-doored-redistribution threat at app stores and from sideload sources.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5 covers MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 via one dedicated primary trace and three supporting traces. SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-TAMPER (primary, new in v0.5) is the dedicated Constitutional control for runtime anti-tampering — code and resource integrity checks where supported, signed-build identity verification through platform-supported mechanisms, modified-package detection, and declared tamper-response policy (terminate, degraded mode, alert-and-continue). SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-DEBUG (supporting) covers the runtime debugger-detection vector of tampering — debuggers are a common tampering instrument. SEC-PILLAR-5-JAILBREAK-DETECTION (supporting) covers the tampered-platform vector — a jailbroken/rooted device is a tampering-enabling environment. SEC-PILLAR-5-PLATFORM-ATTESTATION (supporting) covers the cryptographic-evidence vector — attestation tokens provide signed assertions of unmodified app + device state. The mapping is partial because RESILIENCE-2's anti-tampering scope spans multiple vectors — code/resource integrity (ANTI-TAMPER primary), runtime debugger interference (ANTI-DEBUG), tampered execution environment (JAILBREAK-DETECTION), and cryptographic attestation (PLATFORM-ATTESTATION) — and no single trace covers all four. Implementation correctness for per-platform integrity checks (Apple code signing introspection, Android PackageManager signature verification, embedded checksums for resources), declared tamper-response policy enforcement, and defense-in-depth combination with RESILIENCE-1 + RESILIENCE-3 requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: official-build identity verified through platform-supported mechanisms — App Attest for app instance integrity, App Store receipt and StoreKit transaction validation where applicable, bundle identifier and team identity checks where safely available, and backend-side app-instance verification signals derived from attestation tokens (iOS does not expose a stable in-app SecCode introspection API path equivalent to macOS; runtime self-signature verification on iOS is typically delegated to App Attest + backend verification rather than local SecCode calls)
iOS: bundle resource integrity — checksums for embedded resources (images, plists, JS bundles for hybrid apps) computed at build time, embedded in the binary, verified at runtime
Android: signature verification at runtime — app uses PackageManager.getPackageInfo() with GET_SIGNING_CERTIFICATES, verifying the SigningInfo matches the expected production certificate hash
Android: package-name verification — app checks that its own packageName matches the expected value (defeats simple repackaging attacks that rename the app)

Android: install-source verification where appropriate — app uses PackageManager.getInstallerPackageName() to verify install source matches expected store (Play Store, declared MDM, etc.) when the threat model excludes sideload
security_manifest declares tamper-response policy: which detection triggers fire which response (terminate / degraded read-only mode / alert-and-continue / silent telemetry)

Tamper-response enforced via cryptographic gating where possible — sensitive operations gated by integrity-verified state rather than client-side boolean flag (combine with PLATFORM-ATTESTATION for backend-verifiable integrity)
failure_indicators: Single-mechanism anti-tampering — only one check (e.g., only checking signing certificate hash) — defeated by attackers who patch out that one check
Tampering detection result used as a client-side boolean flag without cryptographic gating — vulnerable to runtime bypass via dynamic instrumentation flipping the flag (Frida / Cycript / Xposed)

iOS: signature/identity check via easily-patched comparison (e.g., NSString equality on team identifier or bundle identifier strings) — attacker patches the comparison to always return true; needs cryptographic verification chain through App Attest + backend, not local string equality alone
Android: signature check using deprecated PackageInfo.signatures field — should use signingInfo (API 28+) for v3 signature scheme; v3 supports key rotation properly
Anti-tamper checks executed once at app launch only — attacker bypasses with one-time hook; needs randomized re-checks throughout app lifetime, especially before sensitive operations
No declared tamper-response policy in security_manifest — detection without response is observation, not protection

Anti-tamper logic shipped in plain Swift/Kotlin without ANTI-STATIC-ANALYSIS protections — attacker reads detection logic out of the binary and disables it (RESILIENCE-2 without RESILIENCE-3 is brittle)
Resource integrity not protected — attacker modifies images / strings / JS bundles without changing code signature; if no resource checksums, the modification ships undetected
false_positive_traps: Anti-tampering handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately stub signature checks; these are not production violations.

Developer / internal-distribution builds that disable anti-tampering checks (e.g., for QA on rapidly-changing debug builds) are NOT violations as long as the disablement is gated on a build-config flag that is verifiably false in production builds — security_manifest must declare the build-variant scoping.

Apps that intentionally accept tampering risk (e.g., open-source apps with documented redistribution allowance, internal-only utilities with no sensitive data) are NOT violations if the threat model explicitly accepts this — security_manifest must declare the accepted-risk posture; defaulting to no protection without declaration is the violation.

Apps distributed exclusively via the official App Store / Play Store with Apple/Google's platform-level tamper protections may have minimal in-app anti-tampering because store-distribution adds the protection layer — security_manifest must declare the distribution-only-via-store posture; sideload-supporting apps need stronger in-app protections.

Alert-and-continue response policy is NOT inherently a violation if it routes through audit telemetry plus optional remote revocation (server-side decision to invalidate session after tamper alert) — terminate-on-detection is one valid policy among several, not the only valid policy.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 anti-tampering discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-TAMPER) as primary evidence toward MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 coverage on the code-and-resource-integrity vector
uses Constitution Pillar 5 anti-debug discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-DEBUG) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 coverage on the runtime-debugger-tampering vector
uses Constitution Pillar 5 jailbreak-detection discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-JAILBREAK-DETECTION) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 coverage on the tampered-execution-environment vector
uses Constitution Pillar 5 platform-attestation discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-PLATFORM-ATTESTATION) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 coverage on the cryptographic-integrity-evidence vector
declares tamper-response policy in security_manifest (terminate / degraded mode / alert-and-continue) per MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 platform-best-practice requirement
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-RESILIENCE-2. MASVS-RESILIENCE-2 is mapped to four Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-ANTI-TAMPER (primary, dedicated code/resource integrity control) plus three supporting traces covering specific tampering vectors — ANTI-DEBUG (runtime debugger interference), JAILBREAK-DETECTION (tampered execution environment), and PLATFORM-ATTESTATION (cryptographic integrity evidence). Claims about anti-tampering security are valid only when the dedicated primary trace is implemented; supporting-trace evidence alone does not satisfy RESILIENCE-2 because the dedicated ANTI-TAMPER semantics (code/resource integrity checks, signed-build verification, modified-package detection, declared response policy) are mandatory. Defense-in-depth: RESILIENCE-2 protections become substantially more credible when combined with RESILIENCE-1 (platform integrity validation) and RESILIENCE-3 (anti-static-analysis to prevent trivial disablement of detection logic). Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
